title: A 40th Honouring Ritual
subtitle: A ritual for marking a significant birthday as arrival rather than loss
excerpt: A five-part ritual for the 40th birthday or any significant milestone birthday. Holds the grief for what has passed and the recognition of what has been gained in equal measure, and closes with an anchor for the decade ahead.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on life-stage marking traditions and the positive-ageing literature on milestone birthdays. The five-part ritual structure draws on the framework in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use on or near a significant milestone birthday, particularly a decade birthday. Can be adapted for any age.

content.text: A ritual for the significant birthday. Not a bypass of the grief that can come with a milestone age, and not a forced celebration. A ritual that holds both: what the decade cost, and what it gave.
Sit somewhere quiet, ideally on the morning of or the morning after the birthday. Have a pen and paper. This is a solo practice. Ten minutes is enough.
I am ready to release any grief for the decade that has passed and any fear of the decade ahead. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to align with and allow this new decade as a place I arrive, not a place I am pushed into. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Write two sentences on paper: one about what the last decade gave you that you did not expect, and one about what you are bringing into this next one. Keep it short. The sentences are the work.
I am arriving. I have arrived.
Say it aloud. Fold the paper and keep it somewhere you'll find it in ten years.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on life-stage marking traditions across cultures and Erik Erikson's model of adult development. The five-part ritual structure is adapted from the ceremony shape in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).
